variants: Write down your niche in one sentence — the specific problem you solve for a specific type of person — and put it in your phone notes right now.
Open your notes app and lock in your niche: one line, one audience, one problem they have that you can speak to — do it now.
Before anything else, define your niche in a single sentence (who + their problem + your angle) and save it as a pinned note.
tasks.why: Every decision for 90 days flows from a clear niche — the narrower it is, the faster you grow.

variants: Pick ONE platform — TikTok, YouTube Shorts, Instagram Reels, or X — where your target audience already hangs out, and create or clean up your account profile right now with a clear bio and profile photo.
Choose your single launch platform based on where your niche audience is most active, then go set up your profile: bio, photo, and a link if you have one — all done in this session.
Commit to one platform today (not two, not three — one), then spend these 4 minutes making your profile look intentional: photo, bio that says who you help, and a clean username.
tasks.why: Platform focus prevents the scattered energy that kills most creators before they get traction.

variants: Search your niche on your chosen platform right now, find 3 creators with 10K–500K followers, follow all 3, and save their most-viewed video to a 'Swipe File' note with 1 line on why it worked.
Go to your platform, search your niche keyword, identify 3 top creators in the 10K–500K range, and for each one note the hook style they use on their best-performing content in a saved doc.
Find 3 creators in your niche (10K–500K followers, same platform you chose), follow them all, then write down the exact hook from their #1 video in a swipe file — 3 hooks total.
tasks.why: Studying proven formats in your niche is the fastest shortcut past the 'figure it out' phase.

variants: Open your niche note from yesterday and write 10 content ideas in 3 minutes — don't filter, just list topics your target audience would actually search for.
Set a 3-minute timer and brainstorm 10 video or post topics inside your niche — write them all down without judging any of them yet.
Pull up yesterday's niche definition and generate 10 raw content ideas in 3 minutes flat — quantity over quality, list everything your audience might want to watch.
tasks.why: A full idea bank removes the daily 'what do I post?' block that kills consistency.

variants: Watch one video from each of your 3 swipe-file creators, note the exact structure — hook, middle, CTA — in 3 bullet points per video in your notes.
Study the format of your 3 saved creators: play each video and write down their exact opening line, the body structure, and how they end — 9 bullets total.
Analyze your 3 swipe-file creators by watching one video each and documenting hook, payoff, and close — write it down so you can steal the structure, not the content.
tasks.why: Format mastery lets you skip years of trial-and-error on structure.

variants: Film your first piece of content today — pick the easiest idea from your list, shoot it on your phone, and post it to your platform even if it's not perfect.
Record and post your first video or post right now using your simplest idea from the brainstorm list — imperfect and published beats perfect and sitting on your camera roll.
Take your easiest idea from today's brainstorm, film it in one take on your phone, and hit post — your first piece goes live today, no editing required.
tasks.why: The first post breaks the psychological barrier — nothing else matters until you've posted once.

variants: Check yesterday's post right now and write down the exact view count, likes, and comments in a tracking note you'll update every day.
Open your post from day 2, record the current stats (views, likes, comments, shares) in a running log — this is your baseline number.
Pull up yesterday's first post and log its performance metrics in a simple note: views, likes, comments — you'll compare every future post against these numbers.
tasks.why: Tracking from day 1 gives you data to make decisions instead of guesses.

variants: Go engage with 10 accounts in your niche right now — leave a specific, thoughtful comment (not 'great post!') on 10 posts from creators or potential audience members.
Spend 4 minutes finding 10 posts in your niche and leaving real comments that add value or ask a genuine question — no generic responses.
Engage with 10 accounts in your niche: open the explore page for your niche keyword and leave a substantive comment on 10 posts — comments that show you actually watched.
tasks.why: Engagement drives algorithm visibility and builds relationships with future followers.

variants: Film and post your second piece of content today — use a different hook style from one of your swipe file creators and keep the video under 60 seconds.
Create and publish content #2: pick a hook format from your swipe file, shoot it in one take, post it — done in this 8-minute window.
Record and upload your second post using a hook you pulled from a top creator in your niche — different format than yesterday's, posted before this session ends.
tasks.why: Back-to-back posting builds the algorithm signal and your own momentum.

variants: Update your tracking log with today's stats for both posts, then write one sentence on which performed better and what's different between them.
Check both posts, log current numbers in your tracker, and note in one sentence what the higher-performing post did differently.
Open your stats tracker, record today's numbers for posts 1 and 2, and write a single observation — even if it's 'I have no idea yet, but post 2 got more views.'
tasks.why: Early pattern recognition, even with small numbers, builds the data instinct you'll need later.

variants: Engage with 10 accounts in your niche today — focus on commenting on posts from people who are NOT creators but are clearly in your target audience.
Do your daily 10-comment engagement round, but today specifically find potential audience members (not other creators) and leave genuine replies on their posts.
Go engage 10 times in your niche — target followers of the top creators, not the creators themselves, and write comments that sound like a peer, not a brand.
tasks.why: Engaging with audience members (not just creators) builds real community and follower pipeline.

variants: Post content #3 today — write your hook first, then shoot, using the format 'Most people think X, but actually Y' or 'Here's why [common belief in your niche] is wrong.'
Create your third piece of content using a contrarian hook structure: challenge a common assumption in your niche, film it, and post it now.
Film and post #3 with a hook that challenges conventional wisdom in your niche — contrarian content stops the scroll better than agreeable content, go post it.
tasks.why: Contrarian hooks get higher click-through and spark comments, which the algorithm rewards.

variants: Download CapCut (free) right now and spend 3 minutes adding captions to one of your existing posts — captions increase watch time by up to 40%.
Install CapCut if you haven't, open one of your first 3 videos, and add auto-captions using the caption feature — post the updated version.
Open CapCut, load one of your first posts, auto-generate captions, clean up any errors, and export — captioned content keeps viewers longer.
tasks.why: Captions are the single fastest upgrade you can make to existing content with zero filming required.

variants: Study the comment sections of your 3 swipe-file creators and write down 5 questions or pain points your audience is asking — these are your next 5 content ideas.
Spend 5 minutes in the comment sections of your 3 benchmark creators, extract 5 real questions people are asking, and add them to your content idea list.
Mine the comment sections of your 3 top creators: read through their posts' comments, pull out 5 specific questions or frustrations, and log them as content prompts.
tasks.why: Audience questions are pre-validated content ideas that guarantee someone wants the answer.

variants: Create and post content #4 — answer one of the 5 audience questions you just found, film a direct answer in under 60 seconds, and post it.
Film and publish post #4 by answering one specific question from the comments you just mined — lead with the answer in the first 3 seconds, not the setup.
Post #4 today: pick one of the audience questions from your comment mining, answer it directly on camera in one take, upload it now.
tasks.why: Answer-format content signals relevance to the algorithm and gives your audience a reason to follow.

variants: Update your stats tracker with all 4 posts, then circle the one with the highest view count — that hook or format is your early winner.
Log today's numbers across all 4 posts in your tracker and put a star next to the top performer — you'll post a variation of that format tomorrow.
Pull all 4 post stats into your tracker right now and identify which one has the best views-to-followers ratio — write the hook from that post in your notes.
tasks.why: Your early winner tells you which direction to double down on before wasting time on dead ends.

variants: Engage with 10 accounts in your niche, and today reply to anyone who has commented on your own posts — respond to every comment you've received so far.
Do your 10-comment engagement session, but start by replying to every comment on your own 4 posts first, then engage with 10 external posts.
Open your posts, reply to every comment you've received (even if it's 2), then go engage with 10 more niche accounts — done in 4 minutes.
tasks.why: Replying to comments signals community to the algorithm and turns commenters into loyal followers.

variants: Post content #5 today using a variation of your best-performing hook from the tracker — same structure, different topic, posted before this session ends.
Film and post #5 using the hook style that got the most views so far — keep the same opening formula, switch the subject matter, go.
Create post #5 by remixing your top-performing hook: keep what worked (the opening structure), change the content topic, shoot, and upload.
tasks.why: Doubling down on what already works is how you build momentum faster than constantly experimenting.

variants: Do a full week-1 review: pull all 5 posts into your tracker, rank them by views, write 2 sentences on what the top 2 have in common versus the bottom 2.
Review your first 7 days right now — log all post stats, rank them, and write down what your top performer and your weakest post have different in their hooks.
Week 1 audit: compare all 5 posts side by side in your tracker, identify your #1 performer, and write one sentence naming exactly what made it win.
tasks.why: Weekly review converts scattered data into a clear direction for the next 7 days.

variants: Write your content plan for days 8–14 right now: list 7 post topics, one per day, each tied to a specific hook style from your swipe file.
Plan next week's content in 5 minutes: 7 topics, hook style for each, and which of your 3 benchmark creators inspired each format — write it all down.
Build your week-2 content calendar: 7 posts, 7 hooks, 7 topics — spend 5 minutes mapping it out so you never have a blank-page day this week.
tasks.why: Pre-planning eliminates the daily decision that drains your will to post.

variants: Post content #6 today and also engage with 10 accounts in your niche — use your review data to pick the hook style for today's post.
Film and post #6 using insights from your week-1 review, then complete your daily 10-comment engagement before this session ends.
Create and publish post #6 (use your best-performing hook format from the week-1 review), then leave 10 comments in your niche — both done this session.
tasks.why: Review without action is just journaling — post AND engage to put insights to work immediately.

variants: Film post #7 right now using the exact hook from your week-1 top performer, but address a different subtopic — copy the structure, not the content.
Shoot today's post (#7) in one take using your proven hook structure from last week — same formula, new topic, posted before you second-guess it.
Use your #1 hook format from week 1 and apply it to a fresh topic for post #7 — film it now, you already know this structure works.
tasks.why: Systematic repetition of winning formats builds your signature style before you even know what it is.

variants: Go engage with 10 accounts in your niche — today focus on accounts that have been active in the past 24 hours to maximize the chance they see your comment.
Do your 10-comment engagement on posts published in the last 24 hours only — fresh posts get more eyes on your comments than week-old content.
Leave 10 comments in your niche today, but filter to posts from the last day — your comment visibility is highest on recent content, work the algorithm.
tasks.why: Commenting on fresh posts maximizes your comment's visibility window before it gets buried.

variants: Open Canva right now, create a simple branded thumbnail or cover image for your profile (if on YouTube) or a text-graphic post for Instagram or X — use your niche colors and post it.
Spend 8 minutes in Canva building one visual asset for your brand — a channel banner, post cover, or graphic that uses a consistent color palette — then publish or upload it.
Create one branded visual in Canva right now: profile banner, post thumbnail, or text-over-image — pick 2 colors and 1 font and stick to them, then post the asset.
tasks.why: Visual consistency makes your profile look intentional and increases follow rate on first impression.

variants: Update your stats tracker for all posts, then calculate your average view count — write it down as your current benchmark to beat.
Log today's numbers for every post in your tracker and compute the average views — this is your baseline that tomorrow's post needs to beat.
Pull stats on all posts, enter them in your tracker, and find your average view count — write it at the top of your tracking doc as your number to beat.
tasks.why: An average benchmark turns vague 'am I growing?' anxiety into a concrete target.

variants: Watch 3 videos in your niche that each have over 100K views and pause at the 3-second mark — write down the exact opening line of each video in your swipe file.
Find 3 viral videos in your niche (100K+ views), pause each one immediately after the hook, and transcribe the opening sentence word-for-word into your swipe file.
Study 3 high-performing videos in your niche by noting what the creator says in the first 3 seconds of each — write the exact words, not a paraphrase.
tasks.why: The hook is the only part of your video that determines whether anyone watches the rest.

variants: Film and post #8 with a hook you transcribed today — adapt one of the 3 viral hooks to your niche topic, shoot it in one take, publish it now.
Use one of the hooks you just collected and build post #8 around it — adapt the exact hook formula to your topic, film it, post it before this session ends.
Post #8 today: take one viral hook from your research, swap in your niche topic, film the whole thing in one take, upload — zero overthinking.
tasks.why: Applying a proven hook formula immediately compounds the research into a real post.

variants: Engage with 10 accounts in your niche and today specifically reply to comments under your own content — respond to everyone who has commented on posts 7, 8, or 9.
Check posts 7, 8, and 9 for comments and reply to every single one first, then do your 10-comment engagement in your niche — all done in 3 minutes.
Reply to every comment you've received in the past 3 days on your posts, then go engage with 10 external niche accounts before this timer ends.
tasks.why: Consistent reply behavior signals a real community to the algorithm, boosting your reach.

variants: Pick your single best post so far, open it in CapCut, and add one new edit — a text overlay, a sound effect, or B-roll — then re-export and replace it (or post it as a new piece).
Take your top-performing post and upgrade it: add captions, a hook text overlay in the first 2 seconds, or a trending sound in CapCut, then re-export it.
Open your best post in CapCut and add one production upgrade — whether it's auto-captions, a sound, or on-screen text — then export the improved version.
tasks.why: Iterating on your best content teaches you production skills without risking a post that was already working.

variants: Film and post #9 today — create a 'before and after' or 'mistake vs. fix' format in your niche: show the wrong way and the right way in under 60 seconds.
Post #9 using a comparison format: wrong approach vs. right approach in your niche, shot in one take, posted now — this format gets saved and shared.
Create and upload post #9 in a mistake/fix format — show what most people in your niche do wrong, then what to do instead — film it, post it.
tasks.why: Comparison formats get saved at higher rates, which is the strongest signal to the algorithm.

variants: Check yesterday's comparison post stats and compare it to your average — write one sentence noting whether it beat, matched, or fell below your benchmark.
Log post #9's stats in your tracker and write whether it performed above or below your running average — note the format (comparison) next to its number.
Update your tracker with post #9 numbers, tag it as 'comparison format,' and note in one line whether it outperformed your average.
tasks.why: Format tagging in your tracker is how you'll eventually know which of the 5 hook types actually works for your niche.

variants: Spend 4 minutes reading the top posts on Reddit, Quora, or a Facebook group in your niche — find 3 questions people are asking and add them to your content idea list.
Go to Reddit or a niche Facebook group right now, read for 4 minutes, and extract 3 questions that keep coming up — these are your next content briefs.
Search your niche on Reddit or Quora for 4 minutes and pull 3 specific questions or pain points into your content idea bank — real audience language, not your assumptions.
tasks.why: Audience language from forums is more accurate than your guess about what they want.

variants: Post #10 today — answer one of the forum questions you just found, film a direct answer in under 60 seconds, and upload it now.
Film and publish #10 by answering one specific question you pulled from Reddit or Quora — use their exact language in your hook, film it, post it.
Create post #10 using a forum question as your hook — quote the question in the first 3 seconds, then answer it, film and post before this session ends.
tasks.why: Using real audience language in your hook guarantees relevance and stops the scroll for your exact target viewer.

variants: Write a 1-sentence content series concept — 3 to 5 connected videos that build on each other — and note which platform feature you'll use (playlist, series, saved highlight).
Spend 2 minutes drafting a mini-series concept: 3–5 posts that are connected by topic, each teasing the next — write the 3-sentence overview now.
Come up with one content series idea (3–5 episodes) in 2 minutes: pick a topic that can sustain multiple parts and sketch out each episode's angle in one line each.
tasks.why: A series gives followers a reason to come back and the algorithm a pattern to promote.

variants: Engage with 10 accounts in your niche, and for at least 3 of them, reply to a comment someone else left — inserting yourself into existing conversations, not just posting and leaving.
Do your 10-comment engagement today, but on at least 3 posts, respond to an existing comment thread — join the conversation, don't just start one.
Leave 10 comments in your niche today, with 3 of them being replies to other people's comments on popular posts — this puts you in active comment threads with more visibility.
tasks.why: Joining existing comment threads gives you visibility with established audiences, not just the creator.

variants: Film and post part 1 of your mini-series — end the video with 'Part 2 drops tomorrow' to give the algorithm a reason to recommend it and give followers a reason to return.
Post the first episode of your content series today: film it, add a 'Part 2 coming' teaser at the end, and publish it now.
Create and upload mini-series episode 1 right now — keep it under 60 seconds, tell them Part 2 is coming, and post it before this timer ends.
tasks.why: Series content trains your audience to expect new posts and dramatically boosts return visits.

variants: Update your tracking log with yesterday's series episode stats and compare it to your average — note specifically if the series format changes your watch-time numbers.
Log episode 1 of your series in the tracker, tag it as 'series,' and record whether it outperformed your average view count.
Check your series episode's current stats, log them, and write one sentence on whether series content is getting more engagement than your standalone posts.
tasks.why: Tracking series vs. standalone helps you decide whether to build more series or focus on one-offs.

variants: Engage with 10 accounts in your niche today, and also reply to every comment on yesterday's series post — part 1 comments are your warmest audience, treat them accordingly.
Reply to all comments on your series episode first, then go engage with 10 niche accounts — series commenters are the followers most likely to binge the rest.
Do your 10-comment engagement session and prioritize replying to every comment on your series part 1 — these people are engaged enough to come back for part 2.
tasks.why: People who comment on a series episode are the most likely to share it — nurture them.

variants: Film and post episode 2 of your series today — reference episode 1 in the first 5 seconds, deliver the next piece of value, and tease episode 3 at the end.
Post series episode 2: open by calling back to episode 1 ('in part 1 I showed you X, now here's Y'), deliver the value, and tease part 3 — film and post it now.
Create and publish episode 2 of your mini-series — connect it to episode 1 in your hook, give them the next level, and close with a part 3 tease — upload before this session ends.
tasks.why: Connected episodes build watch time across your account, which the algorithm reads as authority.

variants: Do a full 2-week review: open your tracker, list your top 3 posts by views, identify the one hook style that appears in at least 2 of the top 3, and write it down as your primary hook type.
Week-2 audit: review your tracker, rank all posts by views, find the common element in your top 3 performers, and write a 2-sentence summary of what's working so far.
Pull your full 2-week tracking data right now, identify your top 3 posts, and write one sentence naming the format or hook type that got you there — that's your playbook for week 3.
tasks.why: Two weeks of data is enough to spot a real pattern — use it to stop guessing and start systematizing.

variants: Spend 5 minutes planning your hook-testing strategy for days 15–28: list 5 different hook types you'll rotate through (question, statement, number, story, contrarian) and assign one to each post.
Write out your hook-testing plan for the next 2 weeks: 5 hook formats, at least 2 posts each, and what metric you'll use to declare a winner — do it now.
Map out 5 hook types you haven't fully tested yet (question hook, number hook, story hook, statement hook, controversial hook) and schedule which days you'll test each.
tasks.why: Structured hook testing turns the next 2 weeks into data that tells you your single strongest format.

variants: Film and post series episode 3 (completing your mini-series) and do your daily 10-comment engagement in your niche — both done in this session.
Post the final episode of your series and engage with 10 accounts in your niche — close the series with a strong CTA telling viewers to follow for more.
Create and publish your series finale, ending with a clear follow-for-more CTA, then leave 10 comments in your niche — wrap up the series and the day's engagement in one session.
tasks.why: Completing a series builds credibility and your first engaged mini-audience around a connected body of work.

variants: Post #12 today using a question hook — open with a direct question your audience has, answer it in the video, keep it under 60 seconds, publish now.
Film and post your first planned hook test: a question-based hook — start with 'Have you ever wondered...?' or 'Why does everyone in [niche] do X?' and answer it.
Launch your hook-testing phase with post #12: use a question as your opening line, film the video in one take, and post it — question hooks get clicked, go test yours.
tasks.why: Questions trigger psychological engagement — the viewer's brain wants to stay for the answer.

variants: Engage with 10 accounts in your niche today and also DM 2 creators in your niche with a genuine compliment about a specific video — no pitch, no ask, just build the relationship.
Do your 10-comment engagement, then DM 2 niche creators saying exactly which video of theirs you found most valuable and why — build rapport before you ever need anything from them.
After your 10-comment engagement session, send 2 DMs to niche creators: reference a specific piece of their content and say what you learned from it — no CTA, just genuine outreach.
tasks.why: Creator relationships are your biggest leverage point for cross-promotion in days 31–60.

variants: Go back to your stats tracker and tag every post with its hook type ('question,' 'contrarian,' 'number,' 'story,' 'statement') — you need the hook label next to every view count to spot patterns.
Spend 8 minutes retroactively labeling every post in your tracker by hook type so your hook-testing data is clean and comparable from day 1.
Open your tracking doc, go through all 11 posts, and tag each with a hook category — this foundation makes your upcoming hook tests meaningful instead of random.
tasks.why: Clean retroactive data prevents you from drawing false conclusions when you analyze hook performance later.

variants: Log yesterday's question-hook post stats in your tracker and compare it to the average view count for other hook types — write one line on initial performance.
Check post #12's numbers, record them in your tracker under 'question hook,' and note whether it's tracking above or below your overall average.
Update your tracker with the question-hook post's current stats and write one observation — is the question format getting more early views than your baseline?
tasks.why: Early data points on hook performance let you course-correct before wasting a week on a dead format.

variants: Post #13 today using a number hook — open with 'X things you're doing wrong in [niche]' or 'The #1 mistake I see in [niche]' — film it in one take and publish.
Film and post your number-hook test: open with a specific number ('3 things,' 'the 1 mistake,' '5 ways') related to your niche and deliver on it — post it now.
Use a number hook for post #13: lead with a digit in your opening line, film it in one take, and publish — number hooks signal a clear payoff and viewers click through.
tasks.why: Number hooks set a clear expectation, which increases completion rate — the metric that matters most.

variants: Engage with 10 accounts in your niche today, reply to all recent comments on your own posts, then spend 2 minutes reading comments on your top competitor's viral posts to mine new content ideas.
Do your 10 niche engagements, reply to your own comments, then mine 3 new content ideas from the comment sections of your competitors' best posts.
Complete your 10-comment engagement round, respond to your own post comments, and spend the last few minutes extracting 3 content ideas from niche comment sections — add them to your idea bank.
tasks.why: Mining competitor comments gives you validated ideas with zero guesswork about demand.

variants: Post #14 using a story hook — open with a personal moment or observation that leads into your niche topic ('Last week I made a mistake that cost me X...') and post it.
Film and upload a story-hook post: start with a first-person moment from your experience in the niche, pivot to the lesson, keep it under 60 seconds.
Create post #14 with a story opening — one specific moment, real or relatable, that hooks the viewer and leads to your niche insight — film it and post now.
tasks.why: Story hooks create emotional connection — viewers who feel something are more likely to follow.

variants: Spend 5 minutes creating a simple content 'kit' in Canva: a branded text overlay template, a thumbnail template, and a color palette — save them as templates you'll reuse.
Open Canva and build 2 reusable templates: one for text-overlay posts and one for thumbnail graphics — use consistent colors and fonts so your feed looks cohesive.
Build your brand template kit in Canva right now: one post template and one thumbnail template, saved so you can bang out branded graphics in under 2 minutes per post.
tasks.why: Reusable templates slash production time from 20 minutes to 2 minutes per branded asset.

variants: Engage with 10 accounts in your niche and today specifically target accounts with 1K–10K followers — these are your peers, not huge creators, and relationship-building at this tier is more reciprocal.
Do your 10-comment engagement targeting creators in the 1K–10K follower range — mid-tier peers are more likely to follow back and collab than 500K accounts.
Engage with 10 niche accounts today, but only choose accounts between 1K–10K followers — at this tier, a genuine comment often earns a follow or a reply from the creator.
tasks.why: Engaging peer-level creators builds real relationships that pay off in shoutouts and collabs.

variants: Log stats for posts 12, 13, and 14 in your tracker by hook type (question, number, story) and write 1 sentence on which is leading so far.
Update your tracker with the latest numbers for your 3 hook-test posts, tag each by type, and note which hook type is currently winning on views.
Pull the current stats for your question, number, and story hook posts, enter them in your tracker, and write the current hook type leaderboard — even if it's early data.
tasks.why: Mid-test tracking prevents you from making final hook decisions on day-1 data, which is often misleading.

variants: Post #15 today using a statement hook — open with a bold declaration ('Most people in [niche] will never [result] because...') and deliver the explanation — film and post it now.
Create and post #15 with a statement hook: a confident, direct claim about your niche that stops the scroll — 'The reason [audience] fails at X is...' — film and upload.
Use a bold statement as your hook for post #15: make a declarative claim about your niche, back it up in 30–45 seconds, and post it before this timer runs out.
tasks.why: Bold statements create pattern interrupts — the viewer's brain flags them as worth investigating.

variants: Engage with 10 niche accounts, reply to your own comments, and then write one sentence in your content notes on whether posting daily feels sustainable — if not, note what's slowing you down.
Do your 10-comment engagement round, respond to all your post comments, then write a 1-sentence process note: what part of filming and posting takes the longest, and can you cut it?
Complete your engagement session (10 external + all replies on your posts), then spend 1 minute auditing your posting workflow — write down one friction point to eliminate.
tasks.why: Identifying your workflow bottleneck now prevents it from killing your consistency in week 4.

variants: Post #16 using a contrarian hook — challenge a belief that's widely accepted in your niche ('Everyone tells you to do X, but here's why that's wrong') — film and post now.
Film and publish your contrarian hook test: open by directly disagreeing with a popular niche assumption, back it up briefly, and post — contrarian hooks get shared.
Create post #16 with a contrarian opening: pick one commonly held belief in your niche and flip it — film the counter-argument in one take and upload it now.
tasks.why: Contrarian content generates comments and debates, which dramatically increases algorithm distribution.

variants: Go engage with 10 accounts in your niche today and pay specific attention to which style of comments gets replies — note the 1–2 comment types that earn responses in your notes.
Do your 10-comment engagement and track what happens: which types of comments get replies from creators? Write 2 observations in your notes after.
Engage with 10 niche accounts and consciously vary your comment style — some compliments, some questions, some insights — then note which type got a reply.
tasks.why: Learning which comment styles get responses helps you optimize your engagement for relationship building.

variants: Open your content idea bank and cut it down to the 10 strongest ideas by crossing out anything that feels generic, already overdone in your niche, or that you're not excited to film.
Audit your content idea list right now: keep only the 10 ideas you'd actually click on if someone else posted them — delete the rest.
Spend 7 minutes editing your idea list ruthlessly — anything that doesn't have a clear hook, a specific audience, or that you've already seen done better gets cut — keep your best 10.
tasks.why: A curated idea bank means every future post comes from a validated prompt, not a desperate moment.

variants: Update your hook-test tracker with the latest data on all 5 hook types — you should now have view counts for question, number, story, statement, and contrarian — rank them.
Log stats for all 5 hook-type posts in your tracker and rank them by views right now — your current #1 hook type gets used for your next 3 posts.
Pull numbers for all 5 hook-type posts into your tracker and create a simple leaderboard — question vs. number vs. story vs. statement vs. contrarian — write the ranking.
tasks.why: After testing all 5 hook types, you now have real data to stop guessing and start systematizing your best performer.

variants: Post #17 today using your current #1 hook type from the leaderboard — apply it to a topic from your curated idea bank and publish it in this session.
Film and post #17 using the top-ranked hook format from your tracker — execute it with a topic from your top-10 idea list, no experimenting today, just doubling down.
Create and upload post #17 with your winning hook style — pick your best remaining idea and apply the formula that's gotten you the most views — post it now.
tasks.why: Systematically using your top hook type is how you go from scattered results to a predictable growth curve.

variants: Engage with 10 accounts in your niche and today DM 2 of the creators you commented on in days 15–19 with a follow-up — reference your original comment and ask a genuine question about their content strategy.
Do your 10-comment engagement, then DM 2 niche creators you've already engaged with once — move the relationship one step forward by asking a specific question about their process.
Engage with 10 niche accounts and follow up with 2 creators you've already connected with — send a short DM that continues the conversation, no pitch, just genuine curiosity.
tasks.why: Following up on past engagement moves acquaintances to real relationships — the kind that lead to collabs.
